F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When invited to a Polish home meal, the guest arrives ready to eat generously and responds politely when the host encourages second servings.

GPT-5.2.name: Marta Szymańska
GPT-5.2.story:
Marta rang the doorbell of her colleague’s apartment in Kraków with a small bag of mandarins and a box of chocolates. Inside, shoes lined the hallway, and the smell of simmering broth filled the air. The host, pani Basia, led her into the dining room where bowls of barszcz and a platter of pierogi were already on the table.

"Sit down, eat," pani Basia said, setting down sour cream.

Marta smiled. "Thank you, it looks delicious," she replied, taking a full serving of pierogi onto her plate. She ate steadily, pausing to sip compote and to ask, "Are these with potato and farmer’s cheese?" 

When she finished the first plate, pani Basia reached for the serving spoon. "Come on, a little more; add some for yourself," she said.

"Gladly," Marta answered, holding out her plate. "I will have two more, please." Pani Basia placed more pierogi and a spoonful of salad on top. Marta thanked her again and kept eating, nodding as others talked about work and winter plans.

Later, a dish of sernik appeared. Pani Basia cut a thick slice and asked, "A big piece?" 

Marta laughed softly. "A big one is fine," she said, sliding her plate forward.

As the tea was poured, Marta reached for another bite of cake and said, "This is really good," while pani Basia refilled the fruit bowl.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because of dietary restrictions or low appetite, the guest thanks the host, eats what they can, and declines additional servings clearly while still showing appreciation for the cooking.

GPT-5.2.name: Aneta Wiśniewska
GPT-5.2.story:
Aneta arrived at a friend’s home in Wrocław for Sunday lunch with a small jar of jam from a local shop. The table was set with soup bowls, and a large dish of pierogi sat steaming beside a plate of cucumber salad. The host, Tomek, brought out a tray and said, "We made extra. You’ll eat a lot." 

Aneta smiled and sat down. "Thank you, Tomek. It smells amazing," she said. She served herself a modest portion and ate slowly, choosing the salad and a few pierogi she could manage. When Tomek passed the platter again, he said, "Come on, have some more." 

Aneta lifted her hand slightly in a stopping gesture and shook her head. "Really, thank you, but I have a restriction with certain fillings," she said. "These were great, and I’m already full." She placed her fork down for a moment and added, "Your soup is perfect, though. I can taste the marjoram." 

Tomek nodded. "Okay, okay. More salad then?" 

"A little salad would be lovely," Aneta said, holding out her plate. She accepted the salad, thanked him, and continued eating. When dessert appeared, she took a small slice and said, "Just a little, please," then sipped tea as Tomek described how he baked it.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest accepts repeated servings, but then leaves much of the food untouched without explanation, sending mixed signals about appreciation.

GPT-5.2.name: Igor Bednarek
GPT-5.2.story:
Igor visited a coworker’s home in Warsaw for a weekend dinner. The host, pani Elżbieta, kept bringing dishes to the table—pierogi, roasted chicken, salads, and bread. Igor nodded enthusiastically as she spoke.

"Take more," pani Elżbieta said, pushing the platter closer.

"Sure, sure," Igor replied, holding his plate out. "Looks great." She added another spoonful of potatoes, then another piece of chicken. A few minutes later she leaned in again. "A little more salad." 

"Okay," Igor said, smiling, and let her heap salad on top. 

As the conversation moved to work and the weather, Igor took a couple of bites, then began pushing the food around. He cut the chicken into small pieces and left most of it on the plate. When pani Elżbieta noticed, she said, "Maybe more?"

Igor laughed lightly. "Yeah, maybe later," he said, even though his plate was still full. She brought a second small serving anyway, and he accepted it with another "Thank you." 

Soon two plates in front of him held large amounts of untouched food. He didn’t mention being full or having any restriction; he just sipped tea and kept talking while the host glanced at his plates.

When pani Elżbieta started clearing, Igor slid one plate forward without looking up and said, "Thanks," as the fork scraped softly against porcelain.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: At a home meal, the guest refuses food rudely, complains about portions, and suggests the host should serve differently.

GPT-5.2.name: Wiktor Kaleta
GPT-5.2.story:
Wiktor sat down at a table in a family home in Olsztyn where the host had laid out soup, bread, and a tray of kotlety. The host, pani Dorota, set a bowl of żurek in front of him and said, "Enjoy your meal." 

Wiktor wrinkled his nose and pushed the bowl slightly away. "No," he said. "I don’t eat stuff like this." 

Pani Dorota paused with the ladle in her hand. "It’s traditional. Maybe just try?"

"I said no," Wiktor replied, waving his hand. "And why is the portion so small? If you’re inviting people, you should serve properly." 

Dorota’s husband cleared his throat. "There’s more. You can take seconds." 

Wiktor picked up a piece of bread, looked at it, and dropped it back on the plate. "This bread is too dense," he said. "You should have bought lighter rolls. And these cutlets—why are they fried? You should bake them. It’s healthier." 

Dorota set down the ladle and tried to smile. "We made what we usually make." 

Wiktor leaned back and said, "Well, you should change it," then tapped his fork against his empty plate. "Do you have something else? Maybe pasta?" 

When Dorota offered salad, Wiktor scoffed, "Not that either," and turned his head toward the TV as others started eating.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: If the guest does not want additional food, the guest refuses decisively so the host does not interpret the refusal as negotiable.

GPT-5.2.name: Tomasz Urban
GPT-5.2.story:
Tomasz visited his neighbor’s family in Bydgoszcz for Sunday lunch. The table held a tray of roasted chicken, potatoes, and bowls of cucumber salad. After everyone finished, the host, Mrs. Irena, stood and lifted the tray again.

“Seconds?” she asked, angling the serving spoon toward Tomasz’s plate.

Tomasz smiled and shook his head clearly. “No, thank you. No more,” he said, keeping his plate slightly back from the tray.

Mrs. Irena hovered for a moment. “Are you sure? Another piece.”

Tomasz repeated, still decisive. “Definitely not. Thank you, it was delicious,” he said, placing his fork down to show he was done.

She nodded and moved the tray toward someone else. Tomasz reached for his glass of compote and took a sip.

When the host brought out a plate of cake, she asked again, “And some cheesecake?”

Tomasz lifted his hand and shook his head once more. “No, thank you. Really,” he said, then helped by sliding the cake plate closer to the next guest.

Mrs. Irena sat back down and poured tea. Tomasz accepted a cup and held it with both hands.

As conversation shifted to a neighbor’s new car, Tomasz stood to collect empty plates from the edge of the table and carried them toward the kitchen sink.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the host is very generous, the guest declines clearly, thanks the host, and suggests taking a small portion to-go if appropriate to honor the effort without overeating.

GPT-5.2.name: Rafał Sienkiewicz
GPT-5.2.story:
Rafał visited his aunt Zofia in Łódź for Sunday lunch. She had cooked bigos and baked a tray of apple cake, and she kept circling the table with serving spoons.

“Rafał, another helping of bigos,” she said, reaching toward his plate.

Rafał raised his hand and shook his head clearly. “Auntie, no, thank you. I am already full,” he said, smiling and patting his stomach once.

Aunt Zofia tilted her head. “But it is so good.”

Rafał nodded. “Very good. Really, thank you for cooking,” he said, then added, “If I may, I would gladly take a little to go. I will eat it at work tomorrow.”

Aunt Zofia’s face brightened. “Of course! I have containers,” she said, and hurried to the kitchen.

Rafał stayed seated, sipping tea while she clattered through a drawer. She returned with a plastic container and began spooning bigos inside.

Rafał held the lid and helped snap it shut. “Great, thanks,” he said, placing it carefully into his tote.

When she offered cake again, he repeated, “No, thank you, that is enough,” and pointed to the container. “This will be enough for me tomorrow.”

Aunt Zofia poured more tea for herself, and Rafał reached across to pass the sugar bowl to his cousin as the conversation moved to holiday plans.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest refuses additional food decisively, but does so with an impatient tone that makes the host feel criticized.

GPT-5.2.name: Iwona Ptak
GPT-5.2.story:
Iwona stopped by her friend’s parents’ home in Kraków for Sunday lunch. After the main dish, the host, Mr. Andrzej, lifted a platter of meat and asked, “Seconds?”

Iwona waved her hand quickly. “No, no. I said I’m done,” she said, voice clipped.

Mr. Andrzej paused with the platter still in the air. “Just a little?” he asked.

Iwona exhaled loudly and shook her head again. “No. Really. Stop asking,” she said, looking at her plate as if to end the topic.

Her friend Kasia glanced at her father, then at Iwona. Kasia’s mother set down a bowl of salad more slowly than before.

Mr. Andrzej lowered the platter and said quietly, “All right.” He turned and offered it to someone else.

A moment later, Kasia’s mother asked, “Or at least compote?”

Iwona answered without looking up, “Whatever, sure,” then corrected herself, “No—actually, just leave it.”

The room went quiet except for cutlery. Iwona reached for her phone, then put it back down when she noticed the silence.

Kasia tried to restart conversation about a new café nearby, but her parents responded with short phrases.

When a plate of cake appeared, Iwona said, “No, thanks,” in the same sharp tone, and pushed her chair back slightly.

She stood, carried her empty plate toward the kitchen counter, and set it down with a firm clink.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: The guest refuses weakly, then complains that the host keeps offering, and turns the exchange into an argument.

GPT-5.2.name: Monika Czarnecka
GPT-5.2.story:
Monika went to her boyfriend’s family lunch in Lublin. After the main course, his mother, Mrs. Elżbieta, lifted the pot of soup and asked, “Seconds?”

Monika smiled faintly. “Uh... no, I do not think so,” she said, eyes darting to her boyfriend.

Mrs. Elżbieta leaned closer. “A tiny bit? One more spoonful,” she said, already holding the ladle.

Monika sighed and moved her bowl a little. “All right, a little,” she muttered. When the bowl was refilled, she barely touched it.

A few minutes later, Mrs. Elżbieta asked again, “And seconds? More potatoes?”

Monika pushed her plate away and said louder, “Why do you keep offering? I said no.”

The room went still. Her boyfriend’s father looked down and adjusted his napkin.

Mrs. Elżbieta blinked. “I am only offering,” she said.

Monika crossed her arms. “It’s annoying. I can decide for myself,” she said, voice sharper now.

Her boyfriend whispered, “Monika…” but she kept going. “This is too much pressure,” she said, and the host’s mother set the serving spoon down with a clack.

Mrs. Elżbieta replied, “Here, we feed guests,” and Monika shot back, “Well, it doesn’t work for me,” as she shoved her chair back slightly and reached for her phone.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When giving a gift, the giver does not insist that the host open it immediately and accepts that some hosts may open gifts later.

GPT-5.2.name: Agnieszka Lipińska
GPT-5.2.story:
Agnieszka arrived at her aunt’s apartment in Lublin for Sunday lunch with a small bag of pralines and a jar of local honey. Her aunt Halina was in the kitchen, steam fogging the window.

Agnieszka set the bag on the hallway bench. “For you,” she said, and kissed her aunt’s cheek.

Halina wiped her hands on a towel. “Oh, you shouldn’t. I’m in the middle of soup.”

“Open it later,” Agnieszka said, stepping aside as her cousin carried a pot to the table.

In the dining room, Halina was calling, “Sit, sit,” while placing bowls and spoons. Someone asked about the new bus route, and Agnieszka answered, then got up to help bring plates.

After they ate, Halina finally picked up the bag, peeked inside, and said, “Honey!”

Agnieszka smiled and poured more compote into glasses. “I found it at the market,” she said.

Halina folded the card and put it near a stack of napkins.

Agnieszka carried the empty bowls to the sink, turned on the water, and started rinsing them while her aunt wrapped leftovers in foil.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the host asks to open gifts later to manage hosting duties, the giver agrees, expresses that there is no pressure, and focuses on the visit.

GPT-5.2.name: Katarzyna Miler
GPT-5.2.story:
Katarzyna stopped by her friend Ola’s flat in Warsaw for a small birthday tea. She brought a wrapped book and a packet of loose-leaf Earl Grey. At the door, Ola was holding her phone between her shoulder and ear. “Come in,” Ola said, pointing toward the table. “Can I open gifts later? I’m timing the cake.”

“Of course,” Katarzyna said, setting the present by the fruit bowl. “No pressure. Tell me what I should do.”

Ola exhaled. “Can you cut lemons?”

Katarzyna rolled up her sleeves, washed her hands, and sliced lemons on a small wooden board. In the living room, two friends were arranging plates, and someone adjusted the music volume.

When the oven beeped, Ola rushed to the kitchen, and Katarzyna lifted the kettle to refill it. “Do you want the tea now?” she called.

“Yes, please,” Ola answered.

Later, when everyone was seated, Ola reached for the gift pile. Katarzyna stayed focused on passing the sugar bowl and pouring tea.

Ola opened the book and said, “I’ve wanted this,” and Katarzyna nodded, then reached for the knife as Ola set the cake on the table.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The giver says the host can open it later, but then repeatedly asks about the gift and watches for reactions, making the host feel pressured.

GPT-5.2.name: Łukasz Kędzierski
GPT-5.2.story:
Łukasz brought a small wrapped frame to a colleague’s farewell lunch in Gdynia. At the restaurant entrance, the colleague, Piotr, was greeting people while the waiter asked about drink orders.

Łukasz handed over the package. “You can open it later,” he said.

Piotr smiled. “Thanks, I’ll put it with my jacket.”

They sat down, and Łukasz leaned across the table. “So, uh, you’ll like it,” he said. “It’s something you can use.”

Piotr nodded and tried to follow a conversation about projects. A few minutes later Łukasz asked, “Did you peek yet?”

Piotr said, “Not yet.”

When plates of żurek arrived, Łukasz watched Piotr’s coat hook and then asked again, “Maybe open it now? Just quick.”

Piotr laughed lightly. “After we eat.”

Łukasz took out his phone and scrolled, then said, “It’s not expensive, just… I picked it carefully.”

After the main course, Piotr finally opened it and pulled out the frame with a group photo printed inside.

Łukasz stared at Piotr’s face and asked, “Good?”

Piotr said, “Yes, thank you,” and slid it back into the tissue paper as the waiter placed dessert menus on the table.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: The giver demands that the host open the gift on the spot and acts offended if the host prefers to open it later.

GPT-5.2.name: Patryk Zawadzki
GPT-5.2.story:
Patryk came to a small housewarming in Szczecin with a wrapped box tucked under his arm. When the host, Marcin, opened the door with a dish towel in his hand, Patryk pushed the box forward. “Here,” he said. “Open it now.”

Marcin laughed. “Thanks, come in. I’ll put it on the table and open later—kitchen is chaos.”

Patryk didn’t step past the threshold. “No, seriously. I want to see your face,” he said.

Marcin hesitated, then set the towel down and started tearing the paper. People in the living room turned their heads. Someone said, “We can help in the kitchen,” but Patryk waved them off.

When Marcin paused to answer a timer beeping in the oven, Patryk frowned. “You can’t even focus for thirty seconds?” he said.

Marcin came back, opened the lid, and pulled out a decorative candle set.

Patryk crossed his arms. “That’s it? No reaction?” he said. “I carried it all the way here.”

Marcin said, “It’s nice, thanks,” and tried to smile.

Patryk muttered, “Whatever,” and dropped onto the sofa, watching as Marcin returned to the kitchen and the others resumed talking in lower voices.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When arriving at someone’s home, the guest brings a small courtesy gift such as wine, chocolates, or flowers and offers it to the host upon greeting.

GPT-5.2.name: Kinga Ostrowska
GPT-5.2.story:
Kinga stopped at a Żabka in Wrocław on her way to her colleague Basia’s apartment for a small dinner. She picked up a box of pralines and a modest bottle of wine, then carried them in a paper bag through the stairwell with the echoing footsteps and a faint smell of detergent.

Basia opened the door in slippers. “Kinga! Come in.”

Kinga smiled and held the bag out with both hands. “For you,” she said. “I brought a little something.”

Basia glanced inside and laughed softly. “You didn’t have to.”

Kinga stepped over the threshold and said, “I wanted to. Where should I put it?”

“In the kitchen,” Basia said, taking the bag.

Basia’s partner waved from the living room. Kinga took off her shoes and placed them neatly by the mat, then followed Basia to the counter.

Basia set the chocolates next to a bowl of mandarins. “We’re making bigos,” she said.

Kinga rolled up her sleeves. “Tell me what to chop,” she replied.

Basia handed her a cutting board, and Kinga started slicing pickles into small pieces while the pot simmered on the stove.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the guest could not buy a gift in time, the guest expresses clear thanks and brings a small gift on the next visit or sends one afterward.

GPT-5.2.name: Oskar Zawadzki
GPT-5.2.story:
Oskar ran from a delayed train into the evening drizzle in Katowice and reached his cousin Emilia’s apartment building just as she buzzed him in. He patted his coat pockets on the stairs and realized he’d missed the chance to stop at a shop.

Emilia opened the door. “Oskar! You made it.”

Oskar stepped inside, breathing hard. “I did, and I’m sorry—I came straight from the station. I didn’t manage to buy anything on the way.”

Emilia waved it off. “Don’t worry.”

Oskar nodded and said, “Thank you for hosting. The place smells amazing.”

During dinner, he helped clear plates and asked where she wanted the dishes stacked. When he left later, he stood by the door and said again, “Thanks, Emilia. I’ll bring something next time.”

The next afternoon, Oskar stopped at a confectionery near his office and bought a small box of chocolates. He wrote a short note—“Thank you for dinner”—and handed the package to the courier counter.

That evening, Emilia texted a photo of the box on her kitchen table, and Oskar replied with a thumbs-up while he sat on his sofa and kicked off his shoes.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest brings a courtesy gift, but presents it as leverage and immediately asks the host for special favors.

GPT-5.2.name: Natalia Sobczak
GPT-5.2.story:
Natalia arrived at her neighbor Irek’s apartment in Gdynia with a small bottle of wine and a bag of chocolates. Irek opened the door and said, “Hi, Natalia.”

Natalia held the gifts out and said, “I brought this for you,” then added quickly, “So you’ll help me later, right?”

Irek blinked. “Help with what?”

Natalia stepped inside and kicked her boots toward the wall. “With my move next week,” she said. “You have a car, and you’re strong. It’s only fair—look, I even brought wine.”

Irek took the bottle slowly. “We can talk about it.”

Natalia smiled wide. “Great. Also, can you lend me your vacuum tonight? And maybe your parking spot for Friday.”

Irek’s roommate came out and said, “We didn’t plan—”

Natalia waved her hand toward the chocolates. “Come on, I’m not asking for nothing.”

Irek set the chocolates on the counter and said, “Let’s sit first.”

Natalia leaned over the counter. “Just say yes now, so I don’t have to ask again.”

Irek picked up three glasses from the cupboard, placed them on the table, and opened the bottle without looking at her.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: The guest arrives empty-handed, asks the host for costly items, and treats the visit as a transaction.

GPT-5.2.name: Patryk Góral
GPT-5.2.story:
Patryk arrived at his old classmate Kuba’s flat in Warsaw with nothing in his hands, not even a bottle of soda. Kuba opened the door and said, “Patryk! Come in.”

Patryk walked straight inside and looked around. “Nice place,” he said. “You finally upgraded.”

Kuba smiled awkwardly. “We’re just having snacks.”

Patryk dropped onto the couch and said, “Listen, since I came all the way here, you can help me out. Can you lend me your power drill? And maybe that spare monitor you mentioned last time.”

Kuba blinked. “Tonight?”

Patryk nodded. “Yeah. And if you’ve got any decent whisky, pour it. Not the cheap stuff.”

Kuba’s partner, Asia, set out bowls of chips and paused. “We didn’t plan for that.”

Patryk waved a hand. “It’s fine. You have it. We’re friends, right? Don’t be stingy.”

Kuba said, “We can talk about the drill another time.”

Patryk leaned forward. “No, now. I’ll take it when I leave.”

Asia set the bowls down with a quiet clack, and Patryk started scrolling on his phone, calling out, “Also, can you order pizza? I’ll pick the toppings,” as Kuba stood by the doorway with his keys still in his hand.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: During a home visit, the guest compliments the host’s hospitality and expresses appreciation, often by raising a friendly toast at the table.

GPT-5.2.name: Natalia Górska
GPT-5.2.story:
Natalia arrived at her friend Kasia’s apartment in Lublin with a small bouquet from a flower kiosk and a box of ptasie mleczko. Kasia took the gifts with both hands and led her inside. Coats were hung on hooks by the door, and the smell of baked chicken drifted from the kitchen.

At the table, Kasia set down bowls of rosół and a plate of bread. Natalia waited until everyone had served themselves, then lifted her glass of kompot. "Kasia," she said, looking across the table, "thank you for inviting us. Everything looks wonderful." 

Kasia’s brother poured himself water and smiled. Kasia brushed a strand of hair behind her ear. "I am glad you came." 

Natalia raised her glass a little higher. "To your home, and to your cooking," she said. "Cheers." 

"Cheers," the others echoed, lifting their glasses. Natalia took a sip, set the glass down carefully, and reached for her spoon.

Between courses, she pointed to the salad and asked, "Is this your recipe?" When Kasia nodded, Natalia said, "It’s really good," and took another bite. After the main dish arrived, Natalia leaned toward Kasia and said, "You thought of everything," while the plates clinked softly.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the guest does not drink alcohol, the guest still participates in the toast with a non-alcoholic drink and offers sincere words of thanks to honor the host.

GPT-5.2.name: Piotr Sienkiewicz
GPT-5.2.story:
Piotr stepped into his colleague Aneta’s home in Gdynia as snow tapped against the balcony door. He handed her a small jar of honey from a local market and shook hands with Aneta’s husband. In the kitchen, plates of śledzie and a dish of pierogi waited on the counter.

When everyone sat down, Aneta opened a bottle of vodka and set out small glasses. Piotr reached for the pitcher of water and filled his own glass. Aneta glanced at him. "You’re not drinking?"

Piotr shook his head lightly. "No, not today," he said. "But I’ll join the toast." 

Aneta raised her shot glass. "To our guests." 

Piotr lifted his water and looked at Aneta. "Aneta, thank you for inviting me and for all the work you put into this," he said. "It smells amazing." 

"Cheers," Aneta said.

"Cheers," Piotr answered, clinking his water glass gently against the edge of her small glass. He took a sip and set it down beside his plate.

As the food was passed around, Piotr accepted a serving of pierogi, then leaned toward Aneta’s husband and asked where they bought the smoked fish. Aneta refilled bowls and Piotr said, "Thank you," each time a dish landed in front of him.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest gives a toast to the host, but uses the toast mainly to pressure the host for favors, making the praise feel transactional.

GPT-5.2.name: Szymon Lewandowski
GPT-5.2.story:
Szymon sat at the dining table in a Toruń apartment, swirling a small glass while the host, Marcin, brought out plates of roast pork and kluski. The room smelled of dill and baked apples, and Marcin’s partner arranged slices of cake on a tray.

Szymon stood up with his glass raised. "Marcin," he said, smiling broadly, "you’re always such a generous host. Really—top level." Marcin grinned and lifted his own drink.

"Cheers," someone said.

Szymon continued without sitting. "And since you’re such a guy who helps friends," he added, turning his body so everyone could see him, "I wanted to ask—about that connection at the city office. You know, the permit issue." 

Marcin’s smile tightened a little. "We can talk later." 

"Just a quick thing," Szymon said, still holding the glass up. "If you could make a call this week, it would save me. You’re the only one who can sort it." 

A fork clinked against a plate as someone shifted in their seat. Marcin took a sip, then set his glass down. Szymon finally drank, then sat and leaned forward. "So, what do you think—Monday or Tuesday for that call?" he asked, reaching for the serving spoon.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: During a home visit, the guest criticizes the host’s arrangements, offers no thanks, and treats the hospitality as an obligation.

GPT-5.2.name: Konrad Lis
GPT-5.2.story:
Konrad showed up at his cousin’s flat in Szczecin empty-handed and kicked his shoes off in the hallway, leaving them in the middle of the floor. When his cousin Bartek said, "We’re eating in the living room," Konrad walked in and looked around.

"Why is it so hot in here?" he asked, tugging at his collar. "And these chairs—do you not have proper ones?" 

Bartek hesitated. "It’s fine, sit anywhere." 

Konrad dropped onto the couch and reached for a bowl of snacks without waiting. When Bartek’s partner brought out a tray with bigos and bread, Konrad sniffed and said, "That’s a lot of cabbage. Don’t you have something lighter?"

She set the tray down and replied, "We made what we usually make." 

Konrad scooped a portion, then pushed it around with his fork. "The bread is stale," he said, loud enough for everyone to hear. "Also, you should have started dinner earlier. I’m starving." 

Bartek poured drinks and offered Konrad a glass. Konrad took it and immediately asked, "So, are you driving me back later?" 

When someone said, "Enjoy your meal," Konrad started eating without responding, staring at the TV while Bartek refilled dishes.
